title: Working with Arrays
description: Fetch and iterate over lists of data returned by an API.

blocks.content: Many API endpoints return **arrays** of objects rather than a single record. For example, fetching a list of products, users, or posts will give you back a JSON array. Once you have parsed the response, you can loop over the array using standard JavaScript methods like `forEach` or `for...of`.

Some APIs wrap their array in an object under a named key (e.g. `data.quotes`), while others return the array directly at the top level. Always check the API documentation or log the response to see what shape it has.

blocks.caption: Fetching 5 quotes — the array lives at data.quotes

blocks.content: `forEach` calls a function once for each item in an array. Inside that function you can access the individual object's properties.

blocks.title: Print each quote

blocks.content: `for...of` is another clean way to loop over an array. It is especially readable when you need to use `await` inside the loop body.

blocks.caption: Using for...of to iterate over the quotes array

blocks.content: You can use `.filter()` to narrow down an array to only the items that match a condition, and `.map()` to transform each item into a new form.

blocks.title: Filter and map
Tip
blocks.content: Always log the full data object first when working with a new API. This helps you understand the shape of the response before writing any loop logic.

blocks.title: Working with Arrays Quiz
blocks.description: Test your knowledge of fetching and iterating over API data.

questions.question: What method calls a function once for each item in an array?
questions.explanation: forEach() calls a callback function once per element in an array, in order.

questions.question: Given the response below, how would you access the array of quotes?
questions.explanation: The array is stored under the 'quotes' key of the response object, so you access it with data.quotes.

questions.question: Which array method returns a new array containing only items that match a condition?
questions.explanation: filter() returns a new array containing only the elements for which the callback returns true.
